course_description:
This course introduces students to foundational ideas and methods from game theory and information economics which are essential for conducting research in various fields of economics. The course covers strategic decision-making in both simultaneous and sequential contexts, and in environments with or without complete information; examples include signaling games, monopolistic screening and agency theory. Finally, the course provides an introduction to mechanism design.

As a continuation of ECON6101 Microeconomics for Honours, this course explores advanced areas of microeconomic theory through a research-oriented lens, focusing on strategic interactions and imperfect information scenarios. The first half introduces game theory as a tool for analysing strategic interactions. The second half examines applications of these concepts to current research areas such as information economics (including screening, moral hazard, and adverse selection), mechanism design, and related topics in strategic behaviour. Throughout the course, students will engage with recent academic literature, present research papers, and develop presentation skills essential for academic and professional research environments.

course_aims: This course aims to provide students with a solid understanding of game theory and mechanism design, thereby equipping them with the tools needed to conduct independent research in theoretical economics and theory-informed applied and experimental research. Students who complete this course should have a foundational understanding of game theory, and will gain state-of-the-art techniques for microeconomic modelling and analysis.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply game theoretic concepts and solution methods to analyse strategic interactions in economic contexts, demonstrating mathematical rigour and logical reasoning.
Determine and justify appropriate theoretical frameworks and equilibrium concepts to model economic scenarios involving strategic interactions.
Critically evaluate the assumptions, predictions, and limitations of game theoretic models when applied to real-world problems involving information asymmetries and strategic behaviour.
Analyse and critique research applications of game theory, assessing how theoretical models contribute to understanding strategic behaviour in economic contexts.
Effectively communicate economic research through oral presentations and written analysis, translating complex theoretical concepts into clear insights for diverse stakeholders.

assessment_details.title: Midsemester Assignment
assessment_details.task_description: An open book assignment involving problem solving and/or short answer questions. Questions can be given on any topic covered prior to the midsemester assignment. Academic Integrity & AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
To be submitted via the course blackboard website.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Review of a Research Article
assessment_details.due_date: 4/09/2025 - 9/10/2025 The presentations will be scheduled individually and held on one of the following dates during the tutorial: Week 6 - Thursday 04/09/25 Week 7 - Thursday 11/09/25 Week 8 - Thursday 18/09/25 Week 9 - Thursday 25/09/25 Week 10 - Thursday 09/10/25
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Overview Students will select and present a research article from a list that will be posted on Blackboard. This INDIVIDUAL presentation assignment requires students to critically analyse the chosen article and communicate the analysis effectively to the class during a tutorial session. Article Selection & Time Slot Reservation Students must reserve both their chosen article AND presentation time slot on Blackboard on a first-come, first-served basis Presentations will take place during scheduled tutorial sessions Presentation Requirements Your presentation must include the following three components: Context & Related Literature Identify and explain the research question addressed in the article Discuss how this research fits within the literature Explain the gap or need this research addresses Analysis Clearly highlight the article's main contribution(s) Explain the significance of the findings or methodological / theoretical insights Discuss the methodology or approach used Future Research Identify limitations and/or unanswered questions from the article Suggest how future research could build upon or extend this work Assessment Criteria Students will be evaluated on: Content Quality (75% weight) Accuracy and scope of analysis, critical evaluation, coverage of all three required components, demonstration of knowledge synthesis and evaluation through responses during the Q&A session related to all three components Presentation Style (25% weight) : clarity of communication, organisation, time management, effective use of visual aids, and ability to respond thoughtfully and confidently to audience questions Presentation Format Presentation duration: ca. 10 minutes Slides are recommended but optional (ca. 3-5 content slides) Q&A session to follow each presentation (ca. 5 minutes) Presentations take place during the chosen tutorial session Important Notes Reserve both your article and time slot well in advance Technical issues (computer problems, internet connectivity) will not be accepted as valid excuses Attendance at your assigned tutorial session is mandatory Academic Integrity & AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Presentation during a tutorial session.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
As this is an in-person presentation, they can only be deferred by a full 7 days (scheduled during the next tutorial session).
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
As this is an in-person presentation scheduled at a specific time, it cannot be submitted late.

assessment_details.title: Research Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: 13/10/2025 The presentation event will be held in Week 11, with the exact date determined based on the availability of guests, students, and location. This will be communicated well in advance on blackboard. The presentation must be ready by Monday, Week 11.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Overview Students present their thesis research in front of an audience including invited guests from industry, academia, and/or the public sector at the city campus. Each presentation should be approximately 10 minutes in duration and demonstrate both comprehensive understanding of the student's research and effective application of presentation techniques covered in the presentation skills workshop. Presentations should be accessible to a general academic audience without specialist knowledge of the topic. The presentation should include and demonstrate: Clear articulation of the research question, methodology, and key findings using language accessible to non-specialists Demonstration of critical understanding of the research context and significance Application of presentation techniques taught in the workshop sessions Evaluation Students will be evaluated on factual accuracy, clarity of communication, organisation and structure, time management, effective use of visual aids, ability to effectively communicate their research to the audience, and more generally the demonstration of presentation skills learned throughout the semester. Academic Integrity & AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
Discretionary extensions are not available for this task.
The maximum extension allowed is 10 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
In the case of an extension, the presentation needs to be held during the next tutorial session (Thursday, Week 12). In this case, the grade will be determined entirely by the course coordinator instead of the expert panel.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Late submission not possible as presentations are in front of invited audience / expert panel.

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Decision Under Uncertainty Expected utility, risk preferences, subjective expected utility, and related concepts Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Normal Form Games Introduction to strategic interactions, normal form games, and related solution concepts Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Extensive Form Games Introduction to extensive form games (games trees, mixed and behavioural strategies) and solution concepts under perfect information (backward induction) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Repeated Games Indefinitely repeated strategic interactions, Folk theorem Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Presentation Skills Training Presentation skills workshop hosted by UQ Academic Skills Advisers Learning outcomes: L05
Games of Incomplete Information I Introduction to Bayesian games and solution concepts (BNE) Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Games of Incomplete Information II Incomplete information in extensive form games, belief consistency, weak PBE, sequential equilibrium Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Screening Screening model, separating equilibrium, non-existence of equilibrium Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Signaling and Adverse Selection Signalling model, single-crossing property, separating and pooling equilibria, Cho-Kreps Intuitive Criterion, The Market for Lemons Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Moral Hazard Principal-agent problem, first-best and second-best contracts Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Student Presentations Event Students present their thesis research in front of invited guests and other students at this symposium-style event. This is a joint activity together with ECON6202. The student presentation is an assessment item for this course. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
Mechanism Design Revelation Principle, incentive compatible direct mechanism, auctions Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Mechanism Design II Revenue Equivalence, Optimal auction, Myerson-Satterthwaite Theorem Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
